title: Off by one
checkLabels.t7: The answer is not typed in
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was. All three loops are off by one.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint2.typed-answer: The letter count is worked out from the name. Tomorrow the name will be different.
hint2.wrong-letters: Bean is four letters. If you get five, the loop runs one time too many.
hint3.wrong-letters: The length is 4, but the indexes are 0, 1, 2 and 3. There is no index 4.
hint4.wrong-letters: Start at zero and run while you are BELOW the length, not up to it.
why.wrong-first: The first letter is not B.
hint2.wrong-first: The first letter is at position zero, not position one.
hint2.wrong-stamps: From zero to ten inclusive is eleven numbers, not ten.
hint3.wrong-stamps: Either start at zero and run below ten, or start at one and run up to ten. Mix the two and you count one too many.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Three loops. The number of letters in the name, the first letter, and ten stamps for the loyalty card.
blocks.1.text: All three are off by exactly one. Nothing breaks.
blocks.2.text: The most common mistake in programming
blocks.3.text: It has a name. Off-by-one. It happens so often that the name is standard everywhere.
blocks.4.text: The reason is simple: people count from one, computers count from zero. The two systems meet in every loop.
blocks.5.quote: Bean has four letters, but the last one is at position three.
blocks.7.text: There is one form used all over the world, and it reads without thinking.
blocks.9.text: From zero, while you are BELOW the length. Not up to it, not less than or equal. Exactly this.
blocks.10.items.0: starts at 0, because the first element is there
blocks.10.items.1: stops BELOW the length, because the last index is one less
blocks.10.items.2: runs exactly as many times as the length
blocks.11.text: The two systems do not mix
blocks.12.text: Either count from zero and run below the limit. Or count from one and run up to the limit inclusive.
blocks.13.text: Mix them — from zero up to the limit inclusive — and you count one too many. That is exactly what the third loop does, and the stamps become eleven.
blocks.15.text: Fix the three loops. Use the standard form everywhere.
blocks.16.text: Change the name to something longer and check that the letter count still comes out right.
